title: Novels for Evangelion Fans
wikitext:
[]: Novels for Evangelion Fans?             Quote Post by beaverteeth92 » Thu Dec 23, 2010 12:18 am Can someone please recommend me a novel to read that's similar to Evangelion for an English paper? The only restriction from my teacher is that it can't be American or British. Also, please don't recommend me anything over 600 pages or anything with a ridiculous number of characters to keep track of, just because I have a ton of other academic stuff to focus on. Thanks. So far, I'm looking into Murakami's "Hard-Boiled Wonderland and the End of the World", Soseki's "Kokoro", Márquez's "One Hundred Years of Solitude", and Sartre's "Nausea." And before you recommend me it, I've already read Camus's "The Stranger".

[]:             Quote Post by Seele00TextOnly » Thu Dec 23, 2010 12:24 am ...

[]:             Quote Post by Combs » Thu Dec 23, 2010 12:38 am You're sure you can't do an analytical piece arguing for/against nietzche/freud?

[]: Re: Novels for Evangelion Fans?             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Thu Dec 23, 2010 2:00 am beaverteeth92 wrote: Can someone please recommend me a novel to read that's similar to Evangelion for an English paper? The only restriction from my teacher is that it can't be American or British.

[]:             Quote Post by beaverteeth92 » Thu Dec 23, 2010 2:50 pm Combs wrote: You're sure you can't do an analytical piece arguing for/against nietzche/freud?

[]:             Quote Post by Nightweaver20xx » Thu Dec 23, 2010 2:55 pm "The Beast That Shouted Love At the Heart of the World" by Harlan Ellison.

[]:             Quote Post by beaverteeth92 » Thu Dec 23, 2010 3:33 pm Thanks, but again, it can't be American or British and it has to be a novel or play.

[]:             Quote Post by TehDonutKing » Thu Dec 23, 2010 6:09 pm No Exit by Sartre.

[]:             Quote Post by beaverteeth92 » Thu Dec 23, 2010 7:04 pm I've read it already and enjoyed it, but it's way, WAY too short to include annotations and enough analysis to fill 6 or more pages.

[]:             Quote Post by Nightweaver20xx » Fri Dec 24, 2010 12:52 am beaverteeth92 wrote: Thanks, but again, it can't be American or British and it has to be a novel or play.

[]:             Quote Post by Carl Horn » Mon Dec 27, 2010 2:34 am You could also consider Foucault's Pendulum by Umberto Eco.

[]:             Quote Post by InstrumentalityOne » Mon Dec 27, 2010 3:58 am Catchuh in the Ryeth or Catcher in the Rye

[]:             Quote Post by beaverteeth92 » Mon Dec 27, 2010 11:28 pm Carl Horn wrote: You could also consider Foucault's Pendulum by Umberto Eco.

[]:             Quote Post by TehDonutKing » Tue Dec 28, 2010 1:19 am InstrumentalityOne wrote: Catchuh in the Ryeth

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Tue Dec 28, 2010 4:26 am TehDonutKing wrote: It's overrated anyway. It's just some kid complaining.

[]:             Quote Post by InstrumentalityOne » Tue Dec 28, 2010 9:59 am TehDonutKing wrote: It's overrated anyway. It's just some kid complaining.

[]: Lovecraft is American, isn't he? Guess he's out.             Quote Post by Killer Bee » Tue Dec 28, 2010 1:16 pm InstrumentalityOne wrote: Catchuh in the Ryeth

[]:             Quote Post by InstrumentalityOne » Tue Dec 28, 2010 1:28 pm What´s Cthulhu doing in R´lyeh?

[]:             Quote Post by C.A.P. » Tue Dec 28, 2010 2:04 pm TehDonutKing wrote: It's overrated anyway. It's just some kid complaining.

[]: Re: Novels for Evangelion Fans?             Quote Post by misatoe » Tue Apr 22, 2025 5:24 am what did you go with in the end?

Also, please don't recommend me anything over 600 pages or anything with a ridiculous number of characters to keep track of, just because I have a ton of other academic stuff to focus on. Thanks. So far, I'm looking into Murakami's "Hard-Boiled Wonderland and the End of the World", Soseki's "Kokoro", Márquez's "One Hundred Years of Solitude", and Sartre's "Nausea." And before you recommend me it, I've already read Camus's "The Stranger".
